_pass: Pass 1 + worked examples v2 + LA trim
title: Reading data displays
deck: Four standard ways to make a data set visible at a glance: bar charts for categorical comparisons, histograms for numeric distributions, box plots for five-number summaries, pie charts for parts of a whole.
objectives: Identify the appropriate chart type for a given data set and question; read and interpret bar charts, histograms, box plots, and pie charts.

content.text: Numerical summaries are powerful but narrow. The mean and standard deviation of a data set give you two numbers; they cannot show you whether the distribution is symmetric or skewed, whether it has one peak or two, or whether a handful of outliers are pulling the central tendency. For those questions, the eye is faster than any descriptive statistic, and the right chart can communicate at a glance what would otherwise take a paragraph of prose.

This section catalogs the four standard data displays you will encounter on the ALEKS review and in every report, study, and news story you read for the rest of your life: bar charts for comparing categorical values, histograms for showing the distribution of a numeric variable, box plots for compact five-number summaries, and pie charts for breaking a whole into its component parts. Each is the right tool for one kind of question; using the wrong one misleads even when the underlying numbers are correct.
5.4.1  Bar charts versus histograms

The visual distinction between a bar chart and a histogram is small but important: bar charts have gaps between the bars; histograms do not. The gap is a signal about the underlying axis. A bar chart sits on a categorical axis — days of the week, ice-cream flavors, candidate names — whose values are discrete labels with no natural ordering or distance between them. The gaps make this visually obvious. A histogram sits on a numeric axis whose values run continuously from low to high, divided into bins (intervals) that touch each other; the absence of gaps signals that the underlying axis is continuous.

content.caption: Figure 5.4. The four standard data displays. Bar charts and histograms look similar but answer different questions; box plots collapse the distribution into five summary numbers; pie charts show parts of a whole.

content.text: Definition 5.6. A bar chart compares categorical values with separated bars (gaps signal that the x-axis is non-numeric). A histogram shows the distribution of a numeric variable with adjacent bars whose widths are equal-sized bins. A box plot displays the five-number summary: minimum, first quartile (Q1), median, third quartile (Q3), maximum. A pie chart shows parts of a whole as slices whose sizes must total to 100%.
5.4.2  Box plots and the five-number summary

The five-number summary of a data set is the sorted-quartile representation: minimum, first quartile (the median of the lower half), median, third quartile (the median of the upper half), maximum. These five numbers carry more information than the mean alone, because they capture both center (the median) and spread (range = max−min; interquartile range = Q3−Q1) in a single compact summary. The box plot (also called a box-and-whisker plot) draws this summary visually: a box spans Q1 to Q3, a vertical line inside the box marks the median, and two “whiskers” extend out to the min and max.

Margin noteThe box plot was popularized by John Tukey in his 1977 book Exploratory Data Analysis. Tukey originally called the boxes “schematic plots”; the “box-and-whisker” nickname stuck because of the visual resemblance. Modern variants include the violin plot (which adds a density estimate along the whiskers) and the bee swarm (which shows every individual data point overlaid).
5.4.3  Pie charts and their hazards

A pie chart divides a circle into slices whose central angles are proportional to the share of the whole each category represents. The total must always be 100%, so pie charts are appropriate only when the categories partition the whole exactly. They are visually striking but read poorly when there are many categories or when several slices are of similar size: the eye is bad at comparing angles, especially for slices smaller than 10% of the whole.
The shape of the chart must match the underlying question. Bars for categories, histograms for distributions, boxes for summaries, pies for parts of a whole.

Common mistakeUsing a pie chart for data that is not part-of-a-whole. Pie charts only work when the categories add up to exactly 100% with no overlap. Trying to show two overlapping groups in a pie chart (e.g., “students who play soccer” and “students who play basketball,” with overlap) produces nonsense, because the “both” group cannot be assigned to a single slice without distorting the others. Use a Venn diagram or stacked bar chart for overlap data.

Example 5.4. Choosing the right display.

For each scenario, identify the most appropriate data display.

(a) A grocery store wants to compare the dollar sales of its five departments (produce, dairy, bakery, meat, beverage) for the past quarter.
(b) A researcher wants to show the distribution of household incomes in a city of 200,000 residents.
(c) A school board wants to summarize the spread of test scores across all fifth-graders in the district in one compact figure.
(d) A budget report wants to show how a $5,000 monthly household budget is divided across rent, food, utilities, and savings.

Solution.

(a) Bar chart. Five categorical departments, comparing dollar amounts. Gaps between the bars communicate the categorical axis.

(b) Histogram. Income is numeric and continuous; binning the incomes into ranges (e.g., $20K–$30K, $30K–$40K) and showing the bar heights as counts reveals the distribution's shape.

(c) Box plot. A single compact summary of center and spread; ideal when the question is about overall distribution rather than individual scores.

(d) Pie chart. The four categories partition the budget exactly (they add to $5,000 = 100% of the budget), so a pie chart is the natural choice.

In the wildEdward Tufte's The Visual Display of Quantitative Information (1983) is the canonical treatment of how to do this well. Tufte's central rule: every drop of ink on the page should communicate information; decorative ink that does not earn its place is a distraction. The four chart types in this section are workhorses, but they are not the only options. Scatter plots (two numeric variables against each other), line charts (a variable over time), and heat maps (a two-dimensional grid of values) round out the standard kit; choosing the right shape for the question is the visualization skill that does the most work.
Exercises

1. Which display is most appropriate for comparing the populations of the 10 largest US cities?

    Show answer

    Answer. Bar chart. The cities are a small set of distinct categorical labels; the comparison is across categories, not a distribution.

  2. Which display is most appropriate for showing the distribution of heights among 1,000 adult males?

    Show answer

    Answer. Histogram. Height is a continuous numeric variable; binning and counting gives the distribution's shape.

  3. What five numbers does a box plot display?

    Show answer

    Answer. Minimum, first quartile (Q1), median, third quartile (Q3), maximum.

  4. True or false: pie chart slices can sum to more than 100% if the underlying data has overlapping categories.

    Show answer

    Answer. False. Pie charts require partition: slices must total exactly 100% with no overlap. Overlapping categories require a different display (Venn diagram, stacked bar, etc.).

  5. Why do bar charts have gaps between the bars while histograms do not?

    Show answer

    Answer. The gap in a bar chart signals that the x-axis is categorical (no numeric distance between values); the lack of gap in a histogram signals that the underlying numeric axis is continuous and the bars touch because their bins do.

  6. A statistician wants to compare the shapes of the test-score distributions for three different schools using a single chart. Which display is most appropriate?

    Show answer

    Answer. Side-by-side box plots (one per school). Each box plot summarizes one school's distribution; lining them up makes comparison of center, spread, and skewness immediate.
More worked examples

content.title: Reading a histogram
content.problem: A histogram of test scores has these bin heights: **40–50: 2, 50–60: 5, 60–70: 12, 70–80: 18, 80–90: 9, 90–100: 4**. How many students took the test? What was the modal bin?
content.solution:
**Total students = sum of all bar heights.**

2 + 5 + 12 + 18 + 9 + 4 = **50 students**.

**Modal bin** = the tallest bar = **70–80**, with 18 students.

Histograms summarize numeric data by binning continuous values into intervals. The shape of the bars tells you about the distribution — here it's roughly bell-shaped, centered in the 70s.

content.title: Histogram vs bar chart — the gap matters
content.problem: Two charts both show vertical bars of different heights. One has bars that **touch** each other; the other has bars with **gaps** between them. Which chart shows continuous data (like exam scores), and which shows categorical data (like favorite flavors)?
content.solution:
**The bars-touching chart is a histogram** — continuous data binned into intervals. The bars touch because the intervals are adjacent (50–60 abuts 60–70).

**The bars-with-gaps chart is a bar chart** — categorical data. The gaps are visual cues that the categories (chocolate, vanilla, strawberry) are *separate* labels, not adjacent ranges.

*The takeaway.* The visual difference is small but the semantic difference is large. A histogram describes *numbers*; a bar chart describes *categories*. Mixing them up is a common student error and a frequent display-design failure in the wild.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Data displays are the visual interface between data and the reader. Bar charts show categories; histograms show binned numeric data; pie charts show parts of a whole; line charts show trends over time; box plots show five-number summaries. Reading the axis labels first prevents the most common misreadings.

content.text: Looking ahead
The four chart types of this section are the descriptive toolkit's visual half.
5.4
